title: Notes & Insights Memory Integration
description:
## Overview
Integrate the existing Notes and Insights systems with the hierarchical memory system to create a unified knowledge management experience. Notes and insights currently exist as separate silos - this epic bridges them with memory for seamless context reconstitution.

## Current State
- Notes: Full CRUD API with 7 types (observation, decision, gotcha, pattern, question, todo, reference)
- Insights: Full CRUD API with 8 types with applicability scopes and application tracking
- Both support folder inheritance
- Both have UI panels in the right sidebar
- Neither integrates with the memory system for context injection

## Goals
1. **Note → Memory Promotion**: Allow converting high-value notes to long-term memories
2. **Insight Retrieval**: Include relevant insights in context injection
3. **Note → Insight Extraction**: Automated extraction of insights from accumulated notes
4. **Unified Search**: Search across memories, notes, and insights together
5. **Cross-referencing**: Link related memories, notes, and insights

## Technical Approach
- Add promotion endpoints to convert notes/insights to memory entries
- Wire insights into SessionContextInjectionService
- Create InsightToMemoryService for automatic conversion
- Add semantic search embeddings to insights table
- Update context injection to include relevant notes and insights

## Success Criteria
- High-value notes can be promoted to long-term memory with one action
- Context injection includes relevant insights based on task context
- Notes can be batch-analyzed to extract insights
- Unified search returns results from all three systems
close_reason: Core success criteria met: note-to-memory promotion works, context injection includes notes and insights. P2 tasks remain for future enhancements.

title: Multi-Agent Coordination: Roles, Handoffs & Communication Protocol
description:
ADAPTED FOR TERMINAL MODEL: Coordination via beads (bd), not direct handoffs.

## Terminal Model Constraints
- Agents run in isolated terminal sessions
- No direct context passing between agents
- No real-time handoffs

## Beads-Based Coordination

### Task Decomposition
Orchestrator decomposes complex task into beads issues:
```bash
# Create parent epic
bd create --title='Implement auth system' --type=epic

# Create subtasks with dependencies
bd create --title='Research auth patterns' --type=task
bd create --title='Implement backend routes' --type=task
bd create --title='Implement frontend UI' --type=task
bd create --title='Write tests' --type=task
bd create --title='Security review' --type=task

# Set dependencies (execution order)
bd dep add backend-task research-task
bd dep add frontend-task backend-task
bd dep add tests-task frontend-task
bd dep add review-task tests-task
```

### Agent Assignment
Orchestrator assigns best agent per task type:
```typescript
const agentAssignments = {
  'research': 'gemini',     // Good at research
  'backend': 'claude',      // Good at complex code
  'frontend': 'claude',     // Good at React
  'tests': 'codex',         // Fast for tests
  'review': 'claude',       // Good at analysis
};
```

### Sequential Execution
```
1. bd ready shows 'research' task (no blockers)
2. Spawn gemini agent
3. Gemini completes, closes issue
4. bd ready shows 'backend' task (research done)
5. Spawn claude agent
6. Continue until all tasks done
```

### Context Passing via Beads
Instead of direct handoffs, context flows through issues:
```markdown
## Previous Work
- Research completed by Gemini (remote-dev-xxx)
- Key findings: Use NextAuth v5, JWT tokens
- See: src/docs/auth-research.md

## Your Task
Implement backend routes based on research.
```

### Parallel Execution (When No Dependencies)
```
bd ready shows multiple tasks
       │
       ├─▶ Spawn agent for task A
       │
       └─▶ Spawn agent for task B (different session)
```

### Blocker Handling
If agent hits blocker:
```bash
# Agent creates blocker issue
bd create --title='Need API credentials' --type=task --priority=0
bd dep add current-task blocker-task
```

Orchestrator sees blocked task, can:
- Resolve blocker automatically
- Assign different agent
- Escalate to user

### Reviewer Pattern
For code review without real-time handoff:
```
1. Implementer closes task
2. Orchestrator creates 'review' task
3. Reviewer agent picks up review task
4. If issues found: creates fix task, depends on review
5. Original implementer (or other) picks up fix task
```

## Role Specialization
Instead of real-time roles, use agent selection heuristics:
```typescript
function selectAgent(task: BeadsIssue): AgentProvider {
  const taskType = classifyTask(task);
  
  switch (taskType) {
    case 'research': return 'gemini';
    case 'complex_code': return 'claude';
    case 'quick_fix': return 'codex';
    case 'review': return 'claude';
    default: return 'claude';
  }
}
```

## Files to Create
- src/services/task-decomposition-service.ts
- src/services/agent-assignment-service.ts
- src/services/dependency-resolver-service.ts
- src/lib/agent-heuristics.ts
close_reason: Implemented beads-based multi-agent coordination with heuristics, decomposition, and assignment services

title: Agent Instrumentation Layer: Skills, Plugins & Config Generation
description:
CRITICAL: Define and generate the artifacts that make agents more effective.

## Goal
Instrument CLI agents (claude, codex, gemini, opencode) to be more efficient by providing:
- Project-specific context (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, etc.)
- Reusable skills (slash commands, workflows)
- Plugins (extended capabilities)
- MCP tools (programmatic access)

## Instrumentation Artifacts

### 1. Config Files (Per-Project)

**CLAUDE.md** (Claude Code)
```markdown
# Project Context
- Tech stack, conventions, patterns
- Common commands
- Known gotchas
- File structure overview

# Skills Available
- /test - Run tests with coverage
- /lint - Lint and fix
- /deploy - Deploy to staging

# Task Management
- Always use bd for tracking
- Check bd ready before starting
```

**AGENTS.md** (Codex)
Similar structure for Codex CLI

**GEMINI.md** (Gemini CLI)
Similar structure for Gemini CLI

**.opencode/config** (OpenCode)
Similar structure for OpenCode

### 2. Skills (Reusable Workflows)

Skills are slash commands agents can invoke:
```typescript
interface Skill {
  name: string;           // e.g., 'test'
  command: string;        // e.g., '/test'
  description: string;
  
  // What it does
  workflow: {
    steps: SkillStep[];
    onError: 'stop' | 'continue' | 'retry';
  };
  
  // When to suggest it
  triggers: string[];     // NL patterns
  
  // Scope
  scope: 'global' | 'project';
  projectPath?: string;
}

interface SkillStep {
  type: 'command' | 'read' | 'write' | 'ask';
  action: string;
  condition?: string;
}
```

**Example: /quality-check skill**
```yaml
name: quality-check
command: /quality-check
description: Run complete pre-commit validation
workflow:
  steps:
    - type: command
      action: bun run lint
    - type: command
      action: bun run typecheck
    - type: command
      action: bun run test
    - type: command
      action: bun run build
  onError: stop
triggers:
  - 'before commit'
  - 'check quality'
  - 'validate code'
scope: project
```

### 3. Plugins (Extended Capabilities)

**Claude Code Hooks** (.claude/settings.local.json)
```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "Stop": [{
      "type": "command",
      "command": ["node", "orchestrator-notify.mjs", "task_complete"]
    }],
    "PreToolUse": [{
      "type": "command", 
      "command": ["node", "validate-tool.mjs"]
    }]
  }
}
```

**OpenCode Plugins** (.opencode/plugin/)
TypeScript plugins that extend OpenCode

**Codex Hooks** (.codex/config.toml)
Event handlers for Codex CLI

### 4. MCP Tools (Programmatic Access)

Tools agents can call for project operations:
- session_* - Terminal session management
- git_* - Git operations
- bd_* - Beads issue management
- project_* - Project metadata

## Generation Pipeline

### From Transcript Analysis
```
Transcript shows:
  - Agent ran 'bun run test' 5 times
  - Agent searched for 'database schema' 3 times

Generate:
  - Skill: /test (runs test suite)
  - CLAUDE.md section: Database schema in src/db/schema.ts
```

### From Pattern Library
```
Pattern: React component creation
  - Always needs: component file, test file, export
  
Generate:
  - Skill: /create-component <name>
  - Steps: create file, add boilerplate, create test, update index
```

### From User Feedback
```
User: 'Add a skill for deploying to staging'

Generate:
  - Skill: /deploy-staging
  - Steps: build, test, deploy
```

## Scope Management

### Global (Master Control)
- ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md - Global context
- ~/.claude/skills/ - Global skills
- Applies to ALL projects

### Project (Folder Control)
- {project}/CLAUDE.md - Project context
- {project}/.claude/skills/ - Project skills
- Overrides/extends global

### Promotion Flow
```
Project skill used in 3+ projects
       │
       ▼
Master Control detects pattern
       │
       ▼
Promotes to global skill
       │
       ▼
All projects benefit
```

## Files to Create
- src/services/instrumentation-service.ts
- src/services/skill-generator-service.ts
- src/services/config-generator-service.ts
- src/services/plugin-generator-service.ts
- src/lib/skill-templates/*.yaml
- src/lib/config-templates/*.md

## Integration
- Transcript analysis → identifies needs
- Pattern library → matches templates
- Generator → creates artifacts
- Deployer → writes to project/global
- Verification → tests artifacts work
close_reason: Agent Instrumentation Layer fully implemented with config, skill, plugin, and orchestration services

description:
## Description

Register all new sdk:* MCP tools in the MCP registry so they are discoverable and callable by agents.

```typescript
// src/mcp/registry.ts
export const sdkTools = {
  'sdk:memory_store': {
    name: 'sdk:memory_store',
    description: 'Store a memory entry in the hierarchical memory system',
    inputSchema: zodToJsonSchema(MemoryStoreInput),
    handler: memoryStoreHandler
  },
  // ... other tools
};
```

### Namespace Convention
All SDK tools use `sdk:` prefix to distinguish from other MCP tools.

### Auth Context
Tools must receive auth context with:
- userId
- sessionId (if in session)
- folderId (if in folder)

## Implementation Location
- `src/mcp/registry.ts`
- `src/mcp/tools/index.ts`

- Verify tools appear in listTools response
- Test schema validation
- Test auth context propagation

- [ ] All 4 tools registered

- [ ] Input validation works
- [ ] Auth context available to handlers

title: Orchestrator-First Architecture
description:
Transform Remote Dev from a terminal manager into an autonomous project orchestrator where Master Control becomes the primary user interface. Users describe tasks in natural language to the orchestrator, which spawns sessions, creates worktrees, assigns agents, and coordinates work autonomously.

## Key Capabilities
1. Spawn Sessions: Create terminal sessions on demand for delegated tasks
2. Create Worktrees: Set up git worktrees for task isolation
3. Assign Agents: Choose and launch appropriate agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode)
4. Inject Context: Send task specifications and relevant context to spawned sessions
5. Monitor Progress: Track spawned sessions and collect results
6. Learn & Adapt: Update project config based on completed work
7. Post-Mortem Analysis: Assess completed tasks and design new tools/agents

## Design Decisions
- Interaction: Orchestrator terminal session as primary interface
- Task Format: Natural language input
- Agent Selection: Auto-select based on task type
- Autonomy: Configurable per project (manual/confirm/full)

## Architecture
See docs/ORCHESTRATOR_FIRST.md for full blueprint

CRITICAL: Analyze agent conversation transcripts to understand what works and improve instrumentation.

## Context
CLI agents (claude, codex, gemini, opencode) run in isolated terminals. We can't pass context directly. But we CAN:
1. Read conversation histories after sessions complete
2. Hooks notify us when sessions complete
3. Use learnings to improve project setup for NEXT session

## Transcript Sources

### Claude Code
- Location: ~/.claude/projects/{project-hash}/*.jsonl
- Format: JSONL with messages, tool calls, results
- Contains: Full conversation including tool usage

### Codex
- Location: ~/.codex/sessions/*.json (TBD - verify)
- Format: JSON conversation log

### Gemini CLI
- Location: ~/.gemini/history/*.json (TBD - verify)

### OpenCode
- Location: ~/.config/opencode/sessions/*.json (TBD - verify)

## Analysis Pipeline

### 1. Transcript Ingestion
When hook fires 'task_complete':
```typescript
interface TranscriptIngestion {
  sessionId: string;
  agent: AgentProvider;
  projectPath: string;
  transcriptPath: string;
  beadsIssueId?: string;  // If working on a bd issue
}
```

### 2. Transcript Parsing
Extract structured data:
```typescript
interface ParsedTranscript {
  // Conversation flow
  messages: Message[];
  toolCalls: ToolCall[];
  
  // Patterns
  commandsRun: string[];
  filesRead: string[];
  filesModified: string[];
  errorsEncountered: Error[];
  
  // Metrics
  totalTurns: number;
  totalTokens: number;
  duration: number;
  
  // Behavioral patterns
  backtracking: number;      // Times agent undid work
  retries: number;           // Same action repeated
  contextSwitches: number;   // Changed approach
  toolFailures: number;
}
```

### 3. Pattern Analysis
Identify what to improve:

**Efficiency Patterns**
- Repeated file reads (should be in context)
- Repeated command runs (should be a skill)
- Long search sequences (should have better CLAUDE.md)
- Unnecessary exploration (project structure unclear)

**Error Patterns**
- Same error multiple times (missing gotcha in docs)
- Tool failures (missing capability)
- Wrong assumptions (missing project context)

**Success Patterns**
- What approaches worked well
- Which tools were most effective
- What context was sufficient

### 4. Improvement Generation
Based on analysis, generate:

**CLAUDE.md Updates**
- Add frequently needed context
- Document discovered gotchas
- Add command shortcuts

**New Skills/Plugins**
- Automate repeated command sequences
- Create tools for common operations

**Beads Templates**
- Better issue descriptions
- Include necessary context
- Add acceptance criteria

### 5. Feedback Loop
```
Transcript Analysis
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Pattern Library │ (what we've learned)
└─────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Config Updates  │ (CLAUDE.md, skills)
└─────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Next Session    │ (agent is more effective)
└─────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Transcript      │ (measure improvement)
└─────────────────┘
       │
       └──────────▶ (repeat)
```

## Files to Create
- src/services/transcript-ingestion-service.ts
- src/services/transcript-parser-service.ts
- src/services/pattern-analysis-service.ts
- src/services/improvement-generator-service.ts
- src/lib/transcript-parsers/claude-parser.ts
- src/lib/transcript-parsers/codex-parser.ts
- src/lib/transcript-parsers/gemini-parser.ts
- src/lib/transcript-parsers/opencode-parser.ts

## Integration with Hooks
The agent-event webhook (task_complete) triggers:
1. Find transcript for session
2. Parse transcript
3. Analyze patterns
4. Generate improvements
5. Apply improvements (or queue for review)

## Metrics to Track
- Efficiency improvement over time
- Error reduction rate
- Token usage trends
- Task completion time trends

title: Implement error-to-gotcha retrieval
description:
## Description

When an error is detected, automatically search for similar gotcha notes from past sessions and inject known resolutions.

## Requirements

### Error Detection Trigger
```typescript
// In error-auto-capture-service.ts
async function onErrorDetected(error: DetectedError) {
  // 1. Generate error signature embedding
  const errorQuery = \`\${error.language} \${error.category}: \${error.message}\`;
  
  // 2. Search for similar gotchas
  const similarGotchas = await semanticSearchNotes({
    query: errorQuery,
    type: 'gotcha',
    minSimilarity: 0.75,
    limit: 3
  });
  
  // 3. If found, inject into session
  if (similarGotchas.length > 0) {
    await injectSystemReminder(sessionId, formatGotchaContext(similarGotchas));
  }
  
  // 4. Always store new error as candidate
  await storeErrorCandidate(error);
}
```

### Gotcha Context Format
```markdown
<system-reminder>
Similar error found in past sessions:

## Known Issue: [gotcha title]
**Resolution:**
[gotcha content with fix]

**Source:** Session [session-id] on [date]
**Confidence:** [confidence score]
</system-reminder>
```

### Similarity Matching
Use semantic similarity with boosting for:
- Same language (+0.2)
- Same error category (+0.15)
- Same file pattern (+0.1)

## Implementation Location
`src/services/error-auto-capture-service.ts`

## Testing Requirements
- Test with known error patterns
- Test similarity thresholds
- Verify injection timing
- Test no-match case

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Similar errors retrieve gotchas
- [ ] Resolutions injected automatically
- [ ] New errors stored for future
- [ ] Performance < 200ms
close_reason: Implemented error-to-gotcha retrieval with semantic search. Added findSimilarGotchas(), findSimilarGotchasForErrors(), formatGotchaContext(), autoCaptureErrorsWithRetrieval(), and getContextForScrollbackErrors() functions. Uses rdv-server's /memory/semantic-search endpoint with graceful fallback when unavailable.

title: Implement sdk:memory_store MCP tool
description:
## Description

Create the `sdk:memory_store` MCP tool that allows agents to store memories directly during sessions.

## Requirements

### Input Schema
```typescript
{
  tier: 'short_term' | 'working' | 'long_term',  // Required
  contentType: string,  // Required - observation, pattern, gotcha, etc.
  content: string,      // Required - the memory content
  name?: string,        // Optional short name
  confidence?: number,  // 0-1, default 0.7
  metadata?: object     // Optional structured data
}
```

### Behavior
1. Validate tier and contentType against allowed values
2. Get userId from MCP auth context
3. Get folderId from current session context
4. Generate content hash for deduplication
5. Call session-memory-service.storeSessionMemory()
6. Return created memory ID and confirmation

### Error Handling
- Invalid tier → return error with valid options
- Missing required fields → return validation error
- Duplicate content → return existing memory (touch access count)

## Implementation Location
`src/mcp/tools/memory/store.ts`

## Testing Requirements
- Unit test with mock session context
- Integration test storing each tier type
- Test deduplication behavior
- Test with each agent provider

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tool registered in MCP registry
- [ ] Stores memories in correct tier
- [ ] Handles all content types
- [ ] Returns meaningful error messages
- [ ] Deduplication works correctly
close_reason: Implemented in Rust at crates/rdv-server/src/mcp/sdk_tools.rs. Tool stores memories with tier, contentType, deduplication via content hash, and proper error handling.

title: Auto Tool Implementation: Generate, Deploy & Register MCP Tools
description:
MAJOR: Not just suggest tools, but IMPLEMENT and DEPLOY them automatically.

## Current Gap
- Post-mortem identifies tool opportunities
- Outputs ToolDefinition schema
- But doesn't actually CREATE the tool

## New Capability
When a tool is recommended:
1. Generate implementation code
2. Write to file system
3. Register with MCP server
4. Verify tool works
5. Add to skill library
6. Tool is now USABLE by agents

## Tool Generation Pipeline

### Step 1: Tool Specification (from post-mortem)
```typescript
interface ToolSpec {
  name: string;
  description: string;
  inputSchema: JSONSchema;
  outputSchema: JSONSchema;
  suggestedImplementation: string;  // Natural language
  evidence: PatternEvidence[];
}
```

### Step 2: Code Generation
Use LLM to generate actual MCP tool code:
```typescript
// Generated: src/mcp/tools/generated/run-type-check-and-fix.ts
import { z } from 'zod';
import { execFile } from 'child_process';

export const runTypeCheckAndFixTool = {
  name: 'run_type_check_and_fix',
  description: 'Run TypeScript type checker and auto-fix common issues',
  inputSchema: z.object({
    path: z.string().default('.')
  }),
  handler: async ({ path }) => {
    // Generated implementation
    await execFile('bun', ['run', 'typecheck'], { cwd: path });
    await execFile('bun', ['run', 'lint', '--fix'], { cwd: path });
    return { success: true };
  }
};
```

### Step 3: Sandbox Testing
- Execute generated tool in isolated environment
- Run against test cases
- Verify no side effects beyond expected

### Step 4: Registration
- Add to MCP registry dynamically
- Or write to generated tools directory
- Hot-reload MCP server (or restart)

### Step 5: Verification
- Call tool via MCP
- Confirm it works as expected
- Add to skill library

### Step 6: Rollback
If tool causes issues:
- Unregister from MCP
- Delete generated file
- Log failure for analysis

## Auto-Generated Tool Types

**1. Command Shortcuts**
- Frequent command sequences → single tool
- Example: 'bun run test && bun run lint' → test_and_lint tool

**2. File Operations**
- Common file patterns → dedicated tool
- Example: 'Create API route with validation' → create_api_route tool

**3. API Integrations**
- External service patterns → API tool
- Example: Frequent GitHub API calls → github_helper tools

**4. Composite Skills**
- Multi-step workflows → workflow tool
- Example: 'Create feature branch, setup, run tests' → start_feature tool

## Files to Create
- src/services/tool-generator-service.ts
- src/services/tool-deployer-service.ts
- src/services/tool-verifier-service.ts
- src/mcp/tools/generated/ (directory for auto-generated tools)
- src/mcp/dynamic-registry.ts (hot-reload tool registration)

## Safety
- Generated tools run in sandbox first
- Cannot access secrets without explicit permission
- Cannot modify system files
- All generated code reviewed in audit log
- User can disable/delete any generated tool

title: rdv CLI expects different tmux session naming than bootstrap service
description:
Architecture mismatch between rdv CLI and bootstrap service:

rdv CLI (master.rs:163) looks for:
  session_name = format!("{}-{}", config.master.session_prefix, "control")
  → Expects: rdv-control

Bootstrap service creates:
  Session.create() with standard naming
  → Creates: rdv-{uuid} (e.g., rdv-cb4d282e-...)

This causes rdv master status to show STOPPED even when Master Control session exists.

Options:
1. Change bootstrap service to use rdv-control naming for Master Control
2. Change rdv CLI to look up session name from database
3. Add a symlink or alias mechanism
close_reason: Fixed by using predictable rdv-master-control session name

title: Beads-Centric Task Coordination: bd as Primary Interface
description:
CRITICAL: Use beads (bd) as the primary coordination mechanism between orchestrator and agents.

## Why Beads
- CLI agents run in isolated terminals
- No direct context passing
- Beads persists across sessions
- All agents can use bd commands
- Human-readable issue tracking

## Orchestrator → Agent Communication

### Task Assignment
Orchestrator creates well-structured beads issues:
```bash
bd create --title='Implement user authentication' \
  --type=feature \
  --priority=1 \
  --body='$(cat <<EOF
## Context
This project uses Next.js 16 with App Router.
Auth should use NextAuth v5 with GitHub OAuth.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] GitHub OAuth login works
- [ ] Session persists across page refresh
- [ ] Protected routes redirect to login
- [ ] User info displayed in header

## Files to Reference
- src/auth.ts (create)
- src/middleware.ts (modify)
- src/app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts (create)

## Constraints
- Use existing shadcn/ui components
- Follow project patterns in CLAUDE.md
EOF
)'
```

### Context Injection via Issue Body
Instead of passing context directly, encode it in the issue:
- Project context
- Relevant files
- Acceptance criteria
- Constraints
- Links to related issues

## Agent → Orchestrator Communication

### Progress Updates
Agents update beads as they work:
```bash
bd update $ISSUE_ID --status=in_progress
bd update $ISSUE_ID --body='Added auth routes, working on middleware'
```

### Completion
```bash
bd close $ISSUE_ID --reason='Implemented auth with all acceptance criteria met'
```

### Blocker Discovery
```bash
bd create --title='Need API key for GitHub OAuth' \
  --type=task --priority=0 \
  --body='Blocked on auth implementation. Need GITHUB_CLIENT_ID and GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET.'
bd dep add $ORIGINAL_ISSUE $BLOCKER_ISSUE
```

## Orchestrator Workflow

### 1. Task Planning
Analyze project needs, create issues:
```typescript
async function planProject(projectPath: string) {
  const analysis = await analyzeProject(projectPath);
  const tasks = await decomposeTasks(analysis);
  
  for (const task of tasks) {
    await exec(`bd create --title='${task.title}' --type=${task.type} --body='${task.body}'`);
  }
  
  // Set up dependencies
  for (const dep of dependencies) {
    await exec(`bd dep add ${dep.issue} ${dep.dependsOn}`);
  }
}
```

### 2. Agent Spawning
Spawn agent to work on ready issues:
```typescript
async function spawnAgentForIssue(issueId: string) {
  const issue = await exec(`bd show ${issueId} --json`);
  
  // Create session in project directory
  const session = await createSession({
    projectPath: issue.projectPath,
    agent: selectBestAgent(issue),
  });
  
  // Agent will see issue via bd ready
  // No direct context injection needed
}
```

### 3. Monitoring
Watch for issue updates and hook events:
```typescript
async function monitorProgress() {
  // Check beads status
  const inProgress = await exec(`bd list --status=in_progress --json`);
  const blocked = await exec(`bd blocked --json`);
  
  // Handle blocked issues
  for (const issue of blocked) {
    await handleBlocker(issue);
  }
}
```

### 4. Post-Completion
After hook fires task_complete:
```typescript
async function handleTaskComplete(sessionId: string) {
  // Find associated beads issue
  const issue = await findIssueForSession(sessionId);
  
  // Analyze transcript
  const transcript = await readTranscript(sessionId);
  const analysis = await analyzeTranscript(transcript);
  
  // Update project config based on learnings
  await applyLearnings(analysis);
  
  // Check if more work needed
  const ready = await exec(`bd ready --json`);
  if (ready.length > 0) {
    await spawnAgentForNextIssue();
  }
}
```

## Agent CLAUDE.md Instructions
Add to every project CLAUDE.md:
```markdown
## Task Management (REQUIRED)

Use beads (`bd`) for all task tracking:

### Starting Work
```bash
bd ready                    # See available work
bd show <id>               # Review task details
bd update <id> --status=in_progress
```

### During Work
```bash
bd update <id> --body='Progress update...'
```

### Completing Work
```bash
bd close <id> --reason='Completed because...'
bd sync                    # Push changes
```

### If Blocked
```bash
bd create --title='Blocker: ...' --type=task --priority=0
bd dep add <original> <blocker>
```
```

## Files to Create
- src/services/beads-coordination-service.ts
- src/services/issue-template-service.ts
- src/lib/beads-client.ts (wrapper for bd commands)

## Integration
- Orchestrator uses bd to create/manage issues
- Agents use bd to claim/update/close issues
- Hooks trigger orchestrator actions
- Transcripts provide feedback for improvement
close_reason: Beads coordination complete: BeadsClient for bd CLI wrapper, TaskCoordinationService for beads+worktree integration. Gastown-inspired: folders=convoys, worktrees=isolation, beads=messaging.

title: Session End Analysis: Handle All Termination Scenarios
description:
Handle different session termination scenarios for learning.

## Session End Types

### 1. task_complete (Hook)
- Agent finished work, waiting for input
- Best case: analyze what worked

### 2. session_end (Hook)
- Session explicitly ended
- Reasons: 'exit', 'logout', 'clear', 'timeout'
- Analyze: was task complete? why did it end?

### 3. User-Closed Session
- User manually closed terminal/killed session
- No hook fires (or fires with reason='killed')
- IMPORTANT: Still analyze transcript
- Questions:
  - Was agent stuck?
  - Did user take over manually?
  - Was agent going wrong direction?
  - Did user give up?

### 4. Error/Crash
- Agent crashed or errored
- Hook fires with event='error'
- Analyze: what caused the crash?

### 5. Stall Detection (Orchestrator)
- Orchestrator detected stall
- May have injected command or paused
- Analyze: why did it stall?

## Analysis for Each Type

### task_complete
```typescript
{
  outcome: 'success' | 'partial',
  learnings: extractSuccessPatterns(transcript),
  improvements: suggestOptimizations(transcript)
}
```

### session_end (normal)
```typescript
{
  outcome: 'completed' | 'abandoned',
  taskProgress: estimateProgress(transcript, beadsIssue),
  learnings: extractPatterns(transcript)
}
```

### user_closed
```typescript
{
  outcome: 'interrupted',
  possibleReasons: inferCloseReason(transcript),
  // Was agent:
  //   - Stuck in loop?
  //   - Going wrong direction?
  //   - Taking too long?
  //   - Making errors?
  improvements: suggestFromInterruption(transcript)
}
```

### error/crash
```typescript
{
  outcome: 'failed',
  errorType: classifyError(transcript),
  rootCause: analyzeRootCause(transcript),
  prevention: suggestPrevention(transcript)
}
```

### stall_detected
```typescript
{
  outcome: 'stalled',
  stallPoint: identifyStallPoint(transcript),
  stallReason: inferStallReason(transcript),
  improvements: suggestUnsticking(transcript)
}
```

## Detecting User-Closed Sessions

Since hooks may not fire for user-closed:
1. Periodic session liveness check
2. If session gone but no hook received:
   - Mark as user_closed
   - Find and analyze transcript
   - Log for learning

## Transcript Location Discovery

When session ends, find transcript:
```typescript
async function findTranscript(session: Session): Promise<string | null> {
  const agent = session.agentProvider;
  const projectPath = session.projectPath;
  
  switch (agent) {
    case 'claude':
      // ~/.claude/projects/{hash}/*.jsonl
      return findClaudeTranscript(projectPath);
    case 'codex':
      return findCodexTranscript(projectPath);
    // etc.
  }
}
```

## Integration with Hooks

Update agent-event handler:
```typescript
switch (event) {
  case 'task_complete':
    await analyzeTranscript(session, 'task_complete');
    break;
  case 'session_end':
    await analyzeTranscript(session, 'session_end', reason);
    break;
  case 'error':
    await analyzeTranscript(session, 'error', context);
    break;
}
```

## Files to Modify
- src/app/api/orchestrators/agent-event/route.ts
- src/services/transcript-ingestion-service.ts (add outcome handling)
- src/services/monitoring-service.ts (add liveness checks)
close_reason: Session End Analysis service implemented with comprehensive handling of all termination scenarios

title: Enhance session start hook with memory retrieval
description:
## Description

Enhance the session start hook to retrieve relevant memories and inject them into agent context, not just output command references.

## Current State

`.claude/hooks/session_start.mjs` currently:
- Stores initial session context as short-term memory
- Outputs memory command reference
- Does NOT retrieve existing memories

## Requirements

### Memory Retrieval at Session Start
```javascript
async function retrieveContextMemories(folderId) {
  // 1. Build context query from project info
  const query = buildContextQuery({
    projectPath: process.cwd(),
    gitBranch: getGitBranch(),
    folderName: getFolderName()
  });
  
  // 2. Semantic search for relevant memories
  const memories = await fetch('/api/sdk/memory/semantic-search', {
    method: 'POST',
    body: JSON.stringify({
      query,
      tiers: ['working', 'long_term'],
      limit: 10,
      minSimilarity: 0.6
    })
  });
  
  // 3. Fetch relevant gotchas
  const gotchas = await fetch('/api/sdk/notes?type=gotcha&folderId=' + folderId);
  
  return { memories: memories.results, gotchas: gotchas.results };
}
```

### Output Format
```markdown
# Session Context

## Project Knowledge
[Retrieved long-term memories]

## Known Gotchas
[Relevant gotchas for this project]

## Recent Context
[Working memories from recent sessions]

## Memory Commands
- `rdv memory remember "text"` - Store observation
...
```

### CLI Integration
Use rdv CLI for API calls (handles auth):
```bash
rdv memory search "project context query" --limit 10 --json
rdv notes list --type gotcha --folder . --json
```

## Implementation Location
`.claude/hooks/session_start.mjs`

title: Integration & E2E Verification
description:
## Overview

End-to-end testing to verify the complete memory system works correctly when all components are integrated.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. Complete Session Lifecycle
```
Create session → Store memories → Close session → New session →
Retrieve memories → Verify context reconstitution
```

### 2. Cross-Session Learning
```
Session A: Encounter error → Resolve it → Close session →
Session B: Encounter same error → Verify gotcha retrieved
```

### 3. Agent Memory Usage
```
Start agent session → Agent stores observations →
Agent searches memories → Agent uses retrieved knowledge
```

### 4. Compaction & Recovery
```
Long session → Context approaches limit → Compaction triggers →
Verify working context preserved → Verify relevant memories retrieved
```

### 5. Multi-Agent Coordination
```
Agent A stores pattern in folder →
Agent B (different provider) retrieves pattern →
Verify cross-agent knowledge sharing
```

## Verification Requirements

### Functional Verification
- [ ] MCP tools callable from all agent providers
- [ ] Semantic search returns relevant results
- [ ] Context injection includes memories
- [ ] Hooks trigger at correct times
- [ ] Cross-session retrieval works

### Performance Verification
- [ ] Session start < 2 seconds with context injection
- [ ] Semantic search < 500ms
- [ ] Memory storage < 100ms
- [ ] Compaction completes without blocking

### Reliability Verification
- [ ] No data loss during compaction
- [ ] Graceful degradation when embedding service unavailable
- [ ] Recovery from database errors
- [ ] Concurrent access safety

### Accuracy Verification
- [ ] Semantic search accuracy > 80%
- [ ] Relevance ranking correct
- [ ] Promotion criteria accurate
- [ ] No false positive gotcha matches

## Test Infrastructure

### Browser Testing
Use Chrome DevTools MCP for UI verification:
- Memory panel displays correctly
- Notes panel shows inherited notes
- Insights panel shows applications

### Agent Simulation
Create test harness that simulates agent behavior:
- Terminal output patterns
- Error sequences

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Performance baselines met
- [ ] No regressions from baseline
- [ ] Documentation of test results

- All feature epics complete

1 week

description:
Critical feature: After task completion, orchestrator performs post-mortem analysis to:
1. Assess how to better set up the project (CLAUDE.md, config files)
2. Identify if new agents, subagents, or tools should be created
3. Design these new agents/subagents/tools if necessary

## Post-Mortem Analysis Components

### PostMortemAnalysisService (src/services/post-mortem-analysis-service.ts)
- Analyze task execution logs and outcomes
- Identify patterns (repeated manual steps, missing abstractions)
- Detect opportunities for automation
- Generate improvement recommendations

### ToolingDesignService (src/services/tooling-design-service.ts)
- Design new MCP tools based on repeated patterns
- Generate agent configurations for specialized tasks
- Create subagent templates for common workflows
- Propose CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md updates

### ProjectConfigUpdater (src/services/project-config-updater.ts)
- Update CLAUDE.md with learned patterns and conventions
- Add new command aliases based on frequently used sequences
- Document discovered gotchas and best practices
- Cross-project pattern promotion to ~/.claude

1. Task completes (success or failure)
2. PostMortemAnalysisService analyzes:
   - Time spent on different phases

title: Self-Modification Engine: Agent Self-Improvement Loop
description:
CRITICAL: Enable orchestrator to modify its own behavior and improve over time.

## Inspiration
- Gödel Agent: Self-referential modification of own code
- Self-Improving Coding Agent: Meta-agent selects best version, implements improvements

## Architecture

### Performance Archive
Track every orchestrator 'version' with metrics:
```typescript
interface OrchestratorVersion {
  id: string;
  version: number;
  config: OrchestratorConfig;
  prompts: Record<string, string>;  // System prompts, task parsing prompts, etc.
  tools: ToolDefinition[];
  metrics: {
    taskSuccessRate: number;
    avgTaskDuration: number;
    avgCost: number;
    agentSelectionAccuracy: number;
    userSatisfactionScore: number;
  };
  createdAt: Date;
  parentVersion?: string;  // What version this improved from
  improvements: string[];  // What was changed
}
```

### Self-Improvement Loop
1. Evaluate current orchestrator on recent tasks (rolling window)
2. Select highest-performing version as 'meta-orchestrator'
3. Meta-orchestrator analyzes:
   - Which tasks failed and why
   - Which agent selections were suboptimal
   - Which prompts led to poor parsing
   - Which tools are underutilized or missing
4. Generate improvements:
   - Modify system prompts
   - Adjust agent selection heuristics
   - Create new tools (see Skill Library task)
   - Update task parsing logic
5. Create new version with changes
6. A/B test new version on subset of tasks
7. If better, promote to active; if worse, rollback

### Modifiable Components
- Task parsing prompts
- Agent selection criteria
- Context injection templates
- Post-mortem analysis prompts
- Tool configurations
- Autonomy thresholds

### Safety Constraints
- Cannot modify core safety rules
- Cannot disable oversight
- Cannot increase autonomy beyond user-set limit
- All modifications logged to audit trail
- User can rollback any version

## Files to Create
- src/domain/entities/OrchestratorVersion.ts
- src/services/self-improvement-service.ts
- src/services/version-archive-service.ts
- src/services/ab-testing-service.ts
- src/workers/self-improvement-worker.ts (runs periodically)

## Integration
- Runs after every N tasks (configurable, default 10)
- Or on-demand via API/UI
- Results visible in orchestrator dashboard

title: Memory Consolidation & Compression
description:
## Overview

Implement automatic memory lifecycle management including tier promotion, relevance decay, working memory compaction, and pruning.

## Background

Per CCA paper, memories flow through tiers based on usage patterns. Frequently accessed, high-confidence memories promote to long-term. Unused memories decay and are eventually pruned.

## Current State

- Promotion logic exists but only runs on session close
- No scheduled consolidation
- No working memory compaction
- TTL-based expiry only (no relevance decay)

## Scope

### In Scope
- Consolidation scheduler (runs periodically)
- Promotion criteria refinement
- Demotion logic
- Working memory compaction
- Relevance decay algorithm
- Pruning policies

### Out of Scope
- MCP tools (Epic 1)
- Semantic retrieval (Epic 3)
- Trajectory distillation (Epic 5)

## Consolidation Cycle

```
Every 4 hours:
  1. Cleanup expired short-term memories
  2. Evaluate working → long-term promotion
  3. Evaluate long-term → working demotion
  4. Compact working memory (summarize similar)
  5. Apply relevance decay
  6. Generate missing embeddings
```

## Promotion Criteria

### Working → Long-term
ALL of:
- confidence >= 0.8
- accessCount >= 3
- contentType in [pattern, gotcha, convention, skill]

PLUS ONE of:
- agentValidated (marked as valuable)
- crossSessionUse (used in 2+ sessions)
- userFeedback == positive

### Demotion Criteria
- relevance < 0.3 AND confidence < 0.4
- No access in 90 days
- User explicit demotion

## Compaction Algorithm

```typescript
async function compactWorkingMemory(sessionId: string) {
  const memories = await getWorkingMemories(sessionId);
  
  // 1. Cluster by semantic similarity
  const clusters = clusterBySimilarity(memories, threshold: 0.8);
  
  // 2. Summarize each cluster
  const summaries = await Promise.all(
    clusters.map(c => summarize(c))
  );
  
  // 3. Replace with summaries
  await replaceWithSummaries(clusters, summaries);
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Consolidation runs on schedule
- [ ] Valuable memories promoted automatically
- [ ] Stale memories demoted/pruned
- [ ] Working memory stays bounded
- [ ] No data loss from premature pruning

## Dependencies
- Epic 1: Core Infrastructure

## Estimated Effort
1 week

title: Semantic Retrieval Integration
description:
## Overview

Wire the existing embedding infrastructure to the context injection pipeline, enabling intelligent memory retrieval based on semantic similarity rather than simple text matching.

## Current State

- Embedding service exists and works (all-MiniLM-L6-v2)
- Semantic search API exists in Rust backend
- Context injection uses SQL LIKE queries instead of vectors
- No semantic matching for context retrieval

## Scope

### In Scope
- Wire embeddings to context injection service
- Replace text search with semantic similarity
- Implement query expansion for better retrieval
- Add relevance feedback loop

### Out of Scope
- MCP tools (Epic 1)
- Cross-session retrieval (Epic 4)
- Vector database migration

## Key Changes

### Context Injection Pipeline
```
Current:
  Session Start → SQL LIKE query → Basic filtering → Context file

Target:
  Session Start → Generate query embedding → Semantic search → 
  Re-rank by relevance → Context file
```

### Retrieval Strategy
1. Build context query from project + task info
2. Generate embedding for query
3. Search memories with cosine similarity
4. Apply tier and type weights
5. Re-rank and select top-K

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Context injection uses semantic search
- [ ] Retrieval accuracy > 80% for known patterns
- [ ] Performance < 500ms for typical projects
- [ ] Fallback to text search if embeddings unavailable

## Dependencies
- Epic 1: Core Infrastructure (embedding generation)

## Estimated Effort
1 week
close_reason: Core success criteria met: Context injection now uses semantic search via /memory/semantic-search API. SQL fallback implemented. Query construction from folder metadata. P2 enhancements (query expansion, relevance feedback) remain for future iterations.

title: Async Trajectory Distillation
description:
## Overview

Implement a background agent that watches session trajectories and automatically extracts learnings without interrupting the working agent.

## Background

Per CCA paper, a separate "note-taking agent" runs asynchronously to distill session trajectories into structured notes. This enables learning without interrupting agent work.

## Current State

- Monitoring service captures scrollback every 30s
- Error auto-capture detects patterns
- No trajectory analysis
- No decision tracking
- No outcome analysis

## Scope

### In Scope
- TrajectoryDistillationService implementation
- Hindsight note generation (failure→resolution pairs)
- Decision tracking (why X over Y)
- Outcome analysis (success/fail patterns)

### Out of Scope
- Real-time agent interaction
- Memory consolidation (Epic 6)

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  TrajectoryDistillationService                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
        ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
        ▼                     ▼                     ▼
┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐
│ScrollbackWatcher│   │DecisionTracker│     │OutcomeAnalyzer│
└───────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └───────────────┘
        │                     │                     │
        └─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┘
                              ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │  LearningExtractor  │
                    │  (LLM-powered)      │
                    └─────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │  AsyncNoteCreator   │
                    └─────────────────────┘
```

## Key Components

### Hindsight Notes
Capture error → resolution pairs:
- Error context (message, file, line)
- Resolution steps (commands executed)
- Outcome (success/partial/workaround)
- Time to resolution

### Decision Tracking
Track key decisions:
- Choice made
- Alternatives considered
- Rationale (if discernible)

### Outcome Analysis
Classify session outcomes:
- Task completed successfully
- Partial completion
- Failed/abandoned
- Pivoted to different approach

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Background service runs without interruption
- [ ] Hindsight notes generated for error resolutions
- [ ] Decisions tracked with context
- [ ] Notes stored asynchronously

## Dependencies
- Epic 1: Core Infrastructure
- Epic 4: Cross-Session Learning

## Estimated Effort
1.5 weeks
close_reason: All subtasks completed: TrajectoryDistillationService, hindsight note generation, and decision tracking implemented

title: Create CLAUDE.md memory section generator
description:
## Description

Create a generator that adds a memory system section to project CLAUDE.md files, teaching agents how to use the memory system.

## Requirements

### Generated Section Content
```markdown
## Memory System

This project uses Remote Dev's hierarchical memory system for cross-session learning.

### Automatic Capture
- Errors are auto-captured as gotchas
- Terminal output is monitored for patterns
- Session context is preserved

### Manual Commands
Store observations and learnings:
\`\`\`bash
rdv memory remember "observation"              # Short-term (1 hour)
rdv memory remember -t working "context"       # Working (24 hours)
rdv memory remember -t long "permanent note"   # Long-term (permanent)
\`\`\`

Search past knowledge:
\`\`\`bash
rdv memory search "query"           # Semantic search
rdv notes list                      # View all notes
rdv insights list                   # View insights
\`\`\`

### Best Practices
- Store **gotchas** when you encounter non-obvious issues with solutions
- Store **conventions** when you learn project patterns
- Store **patterns** for reusable solutions
- Store **skills** for useful procedures
- Search memories when starting unfamiliar work
- Use working memory for current task context

### When to Store
| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Found non-obvious bug fix | `rdv memory remember -t long "gotcha: ..."` |
| Discovered project pattern | `rdv memory remember -t long "convention: ..."` |
| Created reusable solution | `rdv memory remember -t long "pattern: ..."` |
| Working on complex task | `rdv memory remember -t working "task context: ..."` |
```

### Generator CLI
```bash
rdv init memory-section [--append] [--path ./CLAUDE.md]
```

### Idempotency
- Check if section exists before adding
- Update existing section if outdated
- Don't duplicate content

## Implementation Location
`crates/rdv/src/commands/init.rs`

## Testing Requirements
- Test with existing CLAUDE.md
- Test with new project
- Test idempotency
- Verify markdown formatting

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Generates valid markdown section
- [ ] Appends to existing CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Creates CLAUDE.md if missing
- [ ] Idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

title: Create agent-specific memory guidance
description:
## Description

Create tailored memory usage guidance for each supported agent provider (Claude, Codex, Gemini, OpenCode).

## Background

Different agents have different capabilities and prompting styles. Memory guidance should be adapted for each.

## Requirements

### Claude Code
- Hooks: session_start.mjs, compact.mjs
- Config: .claude/settings.json
- Instructions: CLAUDE.md
- MCP: Full tool support

### OpenAI Codex
- Hooks: .codex/hooks/
- Config: .codex/config.json
- Instructions: AGENTS.md
- MCP: Limited support

### Gemini CLI
- Hooks: .gemini/hooks/
- Config: .gemini/config.yaml
- Instructions: GEMINI.md
- MCP: Full support

### OpenCode
- Hooks: .opencode/plugin/
- Config: .opencode/config.json
- Instructions: OPENCODE.md
- MCP: Plugin-based

### Guidance Templates
Each agent needs:
1. Hook scripts adapted to their format
2. Config file entries for memory
3. Agent-specific instructions file section
4. Examples using their syntax

### Implementation
```typescript
// Agent-specific templates
const templates = {
  claude: {
    hookDir: '.claude/hooks',
    configFile: '.claude/settings.json',
    instructionsFile: 'CLAUDE.md',
    memorySection: generateClaudeMemorySection()
  },

    hookDir: '.codex/hooks',
    configFile: '.codex/config.json',
    instructionsFile: 'AGENTS.md',

};

title: Vector Storage: LanceDB Integration
description:
Integrate LanceDB as embedded vector database for project knowledge.

## Why LanceDB
- Embedded like SQLite (no server)
- Rust core with TypeScript bindings via napi-rs
- Disk-based queries (scales without memory limits)
- Lance columnar format (efficient storage)
- No compaction required - handles large datasets natively

## Package
```bash
bun add @lancedb/lancedb
```

## Files to Create
- src/infrastructure/vector/LanceKnowledgeStore.ts - LanceDB wrapper
- src/services/embedding-service.ts - Generate embeddings locally

## Schema
```typescript
interface KnowledgeVector {
  id: string;
  type: 'pattern' | 'gotcha' | 'insight' | 'command' | 'tool' | 'agent';
  content: string;           // Text that was embedded
  vector: Float32Array;      // Embedding from model
  confidence: number;        // 0-1
  usageCount: number;        // Times matched during planning
  lastUsedAt: number;        // Unix timestamp
  createdAt: number;         // Unix timestamp
  evidence: string[];        // Task IDs that contributed
  folderId: string;          // For scoping queries
}
```

## Embedding Model
Use local embeddings via @xenova/transformers:
- Model: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dimensions, fast)
- No API calls needed
- ~90MB model downloaded once

## Storage Location
- Per-folder: `data/lance/{folderId}/`
- Global (Master Control): `data/lance/global/`

## Integration Points
- ProjectKnowledgeService: Semantic search for similar patterns
- PostMortemAnalysisService: Store learned patterns
- CompleteTask: Query similar past tasks
- Cross-project: Query global store for common patterns

## Optional Future: Data Hygiene
- Remove patterns unused for 1+ year (optional cleanup job)
- Dedupe near-identical entries (optional)
- Not required for functionality
close_reason: Implemented LanceDB vector storage with embedding service

title: Testing Strategy & Unit Tests
description:
## Overview

Comprehensive testing strategy for the memory system covering unit tests, service tests, and API tests.

## Testing Principles

1. **Test at the right level** - Unit tests for logic, integration tests for flows
2. **Test behavior, not implementation** - Focus on outcomes
3. **Use realistic fixtures** - Test with real-world data patterns
4. **Test edge cases** - Empty states, large datasets, concurrent access
5. **Performance baselines** - Establish and monitor performance

## Test Categories

### 1. Unit Tests (Fast, Isolated)
- Embedding service
- Relevance scoring algorithms
- Decay calculations
- Query expansion
- Content type classification

### 2. Service Tests (Integration)
- Memory storage and retrieval
- Semantic search accuracy
- Consolidation logic
- Context injection pipeline

### 3. API Tests (Contract)
- MCP tool schemas
- REST endpoint behavior
- Error responses
- Authentication flows

### 4. Hook Tests (End-to-End)
- Session start injection
- Compaction reconstitution
- Agent-specific hooks

## Coverage Targets

| Layer | Target Coverage |
|-------|----------------|
| Embedding Service | 90% |
| Memory Service | 85% |
| Context Injection | 85% |
| MCP Tools | 80% |
| API Routes | 80% |
| Hooks | 70% |

## Test Infrastructure

### Fixtures
- Sample memories (each tier, each type)
- Sample embeddings (pre-computed)
- Sample sessions with scrollback
- Error patterns with resolutions

### Mocks
- LLM responses for summarization
- Embedding service for deterministic tests
- Database for isolated service tests

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All new code has tests
- [ ] Coverage targets met
- [ ] CI runs all tests
- [ ] Performance baselines established
- [ ] Edge cases covered

## Dependencies
Each feature epic should have corresponding tests

## Estimated Effort
Ongoing (concurrent with feature development)

title: Implement unified knowledge search API
description:
## Overview
Create a unified search endpoint that queries across memories, notes, and insights.

## Technical Details
- **Endpoint**: `GET /sdk/search`
- **Parameters**:
  - query: Search text
  - types: memory,note,insight (comma-separated)
  - folderId: Scope to folder
  - semantic: Enable semantic search
  - limit: Max results per type

## Implementation Steps
1. Create unified search route in rdv-server
2. Query each system in parallel:
   - Memory: existing semantic/text search
   - Notes: content search + type filtering
   - Insights: title/description search
3. Merge and rank results:
   - Semantic score (if enabled)
   - Recency score
   - Type priority (insight > memory > note)
4. Return unified response with type indicators
5. Add to MCP tools as `sdk:knowledge_search`

## Response Format
```json
{
  "results": [
    {"type": "insight", "id": "...", "title": "...", "score": 0.92},
    {"type": "memory", "id": "...", "content": "...", "score": 0.88},
    {"type": "note", "id": "...", "content": "...", "score": 0.85}
  ],
  "counts": {"insight": 3, "memory": 5, "note": 7}
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Single endpoint searches all systems
- [ ] Results properly ranked and merged
- [ ] Type filtering works
- [ ] Semantic search optional
- [ ] Performance acceptable (<200ms)

title: Agent Memory Awareness & Teaching
description:
## Overview

Implement the multi-layered strategy to teach agents about the memory system and ensure they use it effectively. This is the critical gap - agents currently don't know the memory system exists.

## Background

Per CCA paper, agents need to be taught memory usage patterns. Currently:
- Session start hook outputs commands but agents don't read context.md
- No compaction hook exists for context reconstitution
- CLAUDE.md doesn't mention memory system
- Agents have no guidance on when/what to store

## Scope

### In Scope
- Enhanced session start context injection
- Compaction hook for context reconstitution
- CLAUDE.md memory section generator
- Agent-specific memory guidance
- Memory usage examples and patterns

### Out of Scope
- MCP tools implementation (Epic 1)
- Semantic search changes (Epic 3)
- Cross-session learning (Epic 4)

## 4-Layer Awareness Strategy

### Layer 1: Session Start Context
Automatically inject memory awareness at session creation:
- Relevant memories from semantic search
- Memory command reference
- Known gotchas for current project

### Layer 2: MCP Tools
Direct tool access during sessions (from Epic 1)

### Layer 3: Compaction Hook
When context is compacted:
- Preserve key working context
- Retrieve relevant long-term memories
- Inject as system reminder

### Layer 4: CLAUDE.md Section
Permanent reference in project config:
- Memory commands
- Best practices
- When to store what

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Agents use memory commands in >50% of sessions
- [ ] Context reconstituted after compaction
- [ ] Agents store meaningful observations
- [ ] Memory usage patterns documented

## Dependencies
- Epic 1: Core Infrastructure (MCP tools)

## Estimated Effort
1.5 weeks
close_reason: P1 layers complete: session start hook with memory retrieval (Layer 1), MCP tools from Core Infrastructure (Layer 2), compaction hook for context reconstitution (Layer 3). Layer 4 (CLAUDE.md generator) remains as P2 follow-up work.

title: Implement sdk:memory_search MCP tool
description:
## Description

Create the `sdk:memory_search` MCP tool that allows agents to semantically search across memories, notes, and insights.

## Requirements

### Input Schema
```typescript
{
  query: string,           // Required - natural language query
  tiers?: string[],        // Optional - filter by tier(s)
  contentTypes?: string[], // Optional - filter by type(s)
  minSimilarity?: number,  // 0-1, default 0.5
  limit?: number,          // default 10, max 50
  includeNotes?: boolean,  // default true
  includeInsights?: boolean // default true
}
```

### Behavior
1. Generate embedding for query string
2. Search memories with semantic similarity
3. Optionally search notes and insights
4. Merge and re-rank results by:
   - Semantic similarity (50%)
   - Tier weight (20%): long_term=1.0, working=0.8, short=0.6
   - Content type weight (15%)
   - Recency (15%)
5. Return top-K results with scores

### Response Format
```typescript
{
  results: Array<{
    type: 'memory' | 'note' | 'insight',
    id: string,
    content: string,
    score: number,
    tier?: string,
    contentType?: string,
    createdAt: string
  }>,
  total: number,
  query: string
}
```

## Implementation Location
`src/mcp/tools/memory/search.ts`

## Dependencies
- Embedding service must be working
- Semantic search API endpoint

## Testing Requirements
- Test with various query types
- Test tier and content type filtering
- Verify ranking algorithm
- Test empty results handling
- Performance test with large memory set

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Semantic search returns relevant results
- [ ] Filtering works correctly
- [ ] Ranking produces sensible ordering
- [ ] Response format matches spec
- [ ] Performance < 500ms for typical queries
close_reason: Implemented hybrid semantic + text search in memory_search MCP tool. Generates query embedding, searches similar embeddings using cosine similarity, and combines semantic scores (60%) with text matching (30%) and base relevance (10%). Added 'semantic' parameter to enable/disable semantic search.

title: Implement sdk:insight_extract MCP tool
description:
## Description

Create the `sdk:insight_extract` MCP tool for agents to extract and store project insights.

## Requirements

### Input Schema
```typescript
{
  type: 'convention' | 'pattern' | 'anti_pattern' | 'gotcha' | 'skill' | 'tool',
  name: string,           // Required - short identifier
  description: string,    // Required - summary
  content?: string,       // Optional - detailed explanation
  confidence?: number,    // 0-1, default 0.7
  applicability?: 'project' | 'folder' | 'file' | 'framework',
  applicabilityContext?: string  // e.g., "Next.js", "src/components"
}
```

### Behavior
1. Validate insight type
2. Generate embedding for content
3. Store via insights API
4. Link to source session for provenance
5. Return insight ID

### Insight Lifecycle
- Created with initial confidence
- Confidence increases with positive feedback
- Can be deactivated but not deleted

## Implementation Location
`src/mcp/tools/insights/extract.ts`

## Testing Requirements
- Test each insight type
- Verify applicability scoping
- Test embedding generation
- Test session linkage

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All insight types supported
- [ ] Applicability filters work
- [ ] Session provenance tracked
- [ ] Embeddings generated
close_reason: Implemented in Rust at crates/rdv-server/src/mcp/sdk_tools.rs. Tool extracts insights with type (convention, pattern, gotcha, skill, tool) stored as long-term memories.

title: Cross-Session Learning
description:
## Overview

Enable agents to retrieve relevant knowledge from past sessions when encountering similar problems, particularly for error resolution.

## Background

Per CCA paper, "hindsight notes" capture failure→resolution pairs. When similar failures appear in future sessions, agents should retrieve corresponding notes with known fixes.

## Current State

- Notes and insights exist but are not queried on error
- Error auto-capture creates gotcha notes
- No retrieval of similar past errors

## Scope

### In Scope
- Error → similar gotcha retrieval
- Pattern matching for known issues
- Insight application tracking
- Cross-session provenance

### Out of Scope
- Async note generation (Epic 5)
- Memory consolidation (Epic 6)

## Key Flows

### Error Detection → Retrieval
```
Agent encounters error
       ↓
Auto-capture extracts error signature
       ↓
Semantic search for similar gotchas
       ↓
IF similar found:
  Inject system reminder with known resolution
ELSE:
  Store as new gotcha candidate
```

### Insight Application
```
Context injection retrieves relevant insights
       ↓
Agent applies insight during session
       ↓
Track application (session, timestamp)
       ↓
Collect feedback (helpful/not helpful)
       ↓
Adjust insight confidence
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Similar errors retrieve relevant gotchas
- [ ] Known resolutions injected automatically
- [ ] Insight application tracked
- [ ] Feedback loop improves accuracy

## Dependencies
- Epic 1: Core Infrastructure
- Epic 3: Semantic Retrieval

## Estimated Effort
1 week
